28	
29	- **Safe**: não causa efeito colateral (GET nunca muda dados)
30	- **Idempotente**: fazer 1x ou 10x tem o mesmo resultado
31	
32	> GET NUNCA deve ter efeito colateral. Se perguntarem "por que GET não pode criar algo?", é porque clientes, proxies e crawlers assumem que GET é seguro e podem refazer a qualquer momento.
33	
34	## Status Codes que Importam
35	
36	```
37	2xx — Sucesso
38	  200 OK              → request processado com sucesso
39	  201 Created         → recurso criado (POST)
40	  204 No Content      → sucesso, sem body (DELETE)
41	
42	4xx — Erro do cliente

103	## Event Sourcing (Menção)
104	
105	Em vez de guardar o estado atual, guardar **todos os eventos**:
106	
107	```
108	Event 1: AccountCreated { id: A, balance: 0 }
109	Event 2: Deposited { id: A, amount: 1000 }
110	Event 3: Transferred { from: A, to: B, amount: 300 }
111	Event 4: Deposited { id: A, amount: 500 }
112	
113	Estado atual de A = replay dos eventos = 0 + 1000 - 300 + 500 = 1200
114	```
115	
116	- Audit trail completo (importante em fintech)
117	- Pode reconstruir estado em qualquer ponto no tempo
118	- Mais complexo, mas relevante porque o endpoint se chama `/event`
119	
120	> "Não implementei event sourcing, mas o design do endpoint como /event sugere essa direção. Em produção, cada operação financeira deveria ser um evento imutável num log, o que dá audit trail completo e possibilidade de replay."
121	
122	## Na Entrevista
123	
124	> "Meu design já permite trocar o repositório in-memory por PostgreSQL sem mudar o service. Em produção, usaria transações com SELECT FOR UPDATE pra garantir atomicidade com concorrência real, e ACID do banco como garantia de durabilidade."
125	
126	## Related

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Concurrency & Ownership
2	
3	Concurrency is the ability to handle multiple tasks at the same time — or at least give the illusion of doing so.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Hardware Threads vs Software Threads
8	
9	These are two completely different things that share the same name — a common source of confusion.
10	
11	### Hardware Threads — Fixed
12	
13	Hardware threads are physical — determined by your CPU. This is the true limit of what runs truly simultaneously.
14	
15	```
16	4 cores × 2 threads per core (hyper-threading) = 8 hardware threads
17	```
18	
19	At any given moment, only 8 things can truly run at the same time on this machine. This never changes — it is physical.
20	
21	### Software Threads — Unlimited
22	
23	Software threads are created by your program. You can create as many as you want:
24	
25	```go
26	for i := 0; i < 10000; i++ {
27	    go handleRequest()  // created 10,000 goroutines
28	}
29	```
30	
31	A web server can have thousands of software threads "running at the same time" on a machine with only 8 hardware threads.
32	
33	### The OS Scheduler — The Juggler
34	
35	The OS constantly switches which software thread runs on which hardware thread:
36	
37	```
38	You have 8 "rails"
39	You have 10,000 "trains"
40	
41	The OS keeps switching which train runs on which rail —
42	so fast it looks like all trains are moving at once
43	```
44	
45	```
46	Moment 1:  [req1][req2][req3][req4][req5][req6][req7][req8]   ← 8 running
47	req3 goes to fetch from DB → sleeps, frees the rail
48	Moment 2:  [req1][req2][req9][req4][req5][req6][req7][req8]   ← req9 slotted in
49	req1 finishes → frees the rail
50	Moment 3:  [req10][req2][req9][req4][req5][req6][req7][req8]  ← req10 slotted in
51	```
52	
53	### Why 8 Cores Can Handle 10,000 Connections
54	
55	Most software threads spend **more time sleeping than running**:
56	
57	```
58	req1: received → fetched from DB (slept 10ms) → responded
59	      |← CPU 0.1ms →|←——— sleeping 10ms ———→|← CPU 0.1ms →|
60	```
61	
62	When a thread waits for the database, network, or disk — it is not using CPU. The OS puts another thread on that hardware thread while it waits. This is the key insight behind high-concurrency servers.
63	
64	### Parallelism vs Concurrency
65	
66	| | **Concurrency** | **Parallelism** |
67	|---|---|---|
68	| Definition | Dealing with many things at once | Doing many things at once |
69	| Requires | Just a scheduler | Multiple hardware threads |
70	| Example | 1 chef juggling 10 orders | 10 chefs each cooking 1 order |
71	| Limited by | Design / scheduler | Hardware |
72	
73	> Concurrency is about structure. Parallelism is about execution.
74	
75	---
76	
77	## Single-thread vs Multi-thread
78	
79	### Single-thread
80	The program has only one execution line. Everything runs sequentially — one thing at a time.
81	
82	```
83	task 1 → task 2 → task 3 → task 4
84	```
85	
86	If task 2 takes 5 seconds, tasks 3 and 4 wait.
87	
88	---
89	
90	### Multi-thread
91	Multiple execution lines run in parallel. Each thread handles its own task independently.
92	
93	```
94	thread 1 → task 1 → task 3
95	thread 2 → task 2 → task 4
96	```
97	
98	**Problem:** threads share memory, which creates race conditions — two threads modifying the same data at the same time leads to unpredictable results.
99	
100	---
101	
102	## Event Loop
103	
104	The event loop is the model used by single-threaded runtimes (like JavaScript/Node.js) to handle concurrency without multiple threads.
105	
106	Instead of blocking and waiting, it registers a callback and moves on. When the result is ready, the callback is placed in the queue and executed.
107	
108	```
109	┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
110	│              Call Stack                 │
111	│  (executes one thing at a time)         │
112	└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
113	                 │
114	┌────────────────▼────────────────────────┐
115	│              Event Loop                 │
116	│  (checks if stack is empty,             │
117	│   then picks next from queue)           │
118	└────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
119	                 │
120	┌────────────────▼────────────────────────┐
121	│            Callback Queue               │
122	│  (tasks ready to be executed)           │
123	└─────────────────────────────────────────┘
124	```
125	
126	**How it works:**
127	1. Code runs on the call stack
128	2. When an async operation starts (I/O, timer), it's offloaded
129	3. The stack continues with the next task
130	4. When the async operation finishes, its callback goes to the queue
131	5. The event loop picks it up when the stack is empty
132	
133	---
134	
135	## I/O and Blocking
136	
137	**I/O (Input/Output)** refers to operations that communicate with something outside the CPU — disk, network, database, keyboard.
138	
139	These operations are **slow** compared to CPU execution. The two ways to handle them:
140	
141	### Blocking I/O
142	The thread stops and waits for the operation to complete.
143	
144	```
145	thread: ──── read file ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░ done ──── continue
146	                        ↑ waiting, doing nothing
147	```
148	
149	Wastes CPU time. Common in traditional servers — one thread per request.
150	
151	### Non-blocking I/O
152	The thread starts the operation and moves on. A notification arrives when it's done.
153	
154	```
155	thread: ──── start read ──── other work ──── handle result
156	                                         ↑ callback/signal
157	```
158	
159	Efficient. One thread can handle thousands of connections.
160	
161	---
162	
163	## Go's Approach: Goroutines
164	
165	Go doesn't use an event loop. Instead it uses **goroutines** — lightweight threads managed by the Go runtime, not the OS.
166	
167	```go
168	go doSomething() // spawns a goroutine
169	```
170	
171	**Key differences from OS threads:**
172	- A goroutine starts at ~2KB of stack (OS thread ~1MB)
173	- The Go runtime schedules goroutines across available CPU cores
174	- You can have millions of goroutines running simultaneously
175	
176	```
177	Go Runtime
178	┌──────────────────────────────────────┐
179	│  goroutine 1 → goroutine 4 → ...     │  ← OS thread 1
180	│  goroutine 2 → goroutine 5 → ...     │  ← OS thread 2
181	│  goroutine 3 → goroutine 6 → ...     │  ← OS thread 3
182	└──────────────────────────────────────┘
183	```
184	
185	When a goroutine blocks on I/O, the runtime moves it off the thread and runs another goroutine in its place — so the thread is never idle.
186	
187	---
188	
189	## Ownership & Data Races
190	
191	When multiple goroutines access the same data, a **race condition** can occur — both read and write at the same time, producing unpredictable results.
192	
193	```go
194	// DANGEROUS — race condition
195	counter := 0
196	go func() { counter++ }()
197	go func() { counter++ }()
198	// result could be 1 or 2
199	```
200	
201	### Channels — ownership transfer
202	Go's idiomatic way to share data between goroutines. Instead of sharing memory, you **send** data through a channel — transferring ownership. Think of it like a pipe: one goroutine puts data in, another takes it out.
203	
204	```go
205	ch := make(chan int)
206	
207	go func() {
208	    ch <- 42 // send — blocks until someone receives
209	}()
210	
211	value := <-ch // receive — blocks until something is sent
212	```
213	
214	Both sides block and wait for each other — this is what makes the transfer safe.
215	
216	A common pattern is a **coordinator goroutine** that collects results from many workers running in parallel (fan-out / fan-in):
217	
218	```go
219	ch := make(chan int)
220	
221	// workers run in parallel
222	go func() { ch <- processOrder(1) }()
223	go func() { ch <- processOrder(2) }()
224	go func() { ch <- processOrder(3) }()
225	
226	// coordinator collects all results
227	for i := 0; i < 3; i++ {
228	    result := <-ch
229	    fmt.Println("result:", result)
230	}
231	```
232	
233	```
234	goroutine 1 ──► ch ──┐
235	goroutine 2 ──► ch ──┼──► coordinator (collects results)
236	goroutine 3 ──► ch ──┘
237	```
238	
239	The coordinator doesn't know which goroutine finishes first — it just waits for the next result to arrive. Whoever finishes first sends first.
240	
241	**Does this have a performance cost?**
242	Yes — each send/receive involves synchronization between goroutines. But the cost is minimal in practice. The real bottleneck is almost always the work the goroutines are doing, not the channel itself.
243	
244	> "Do not communicate by sharing memory; share memory by communicating." — Go proverb
245	
246	### Channels vs Mutex
247	
248	| | Channel | Mutex |
249	|---|---|---|
250	| Model | Transfers the data | Shares the data |
251	| Cost | Synchronization on transfer | Lock on access |
252	| Risk | Deadlock if misused | Race condition if misused |
253	| Readability | High — explicit flow | Low — hard to trace |
254	| Best for | Communication between goroutines | Protecting shared state |
255	
256	Use **channel** when goroutines need to **communicate**.
257	Use **mutex** when goroutines need to **share state**.
258	
259	### Mutex — shared access control
260	When you must share memory, use a mutex to ensure only one goroutine accesses it at a time.
261	
262	```go
263	var mu sync.Mutex
264	counter := 0
265	
266	go func() {
267	    mu.Lock()
268	    counter++
269	    mu.Unlock()
270	}()
271	```
272	
273	---
274	
275	## Summary
276	
277	| Model | Thread | I/O | Use case |
278	|-------|--------|-----|----------|
279	| Single-thread + Event Loop | 1 | Non-blocking | Node.js, JS |
280	| Multi-thread | Many | Blocking | Traditional servers |
281	| Goroutines (Go) | Many (lightweight) | Non-blocking | Go servers |
282

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Data Structures
2	
3	A data structure is a way of organizing and storing data so it can be accessed and modified efficiently. The choice of structure directly impacts performance — the wrong one can turn an O(1) operation into O(n).
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Redis Data Structures — When and Why
8	
9	Each structure exists to solve a specific problem. Choosing the wrong one means more código, mais lentidão, e soluções gambiarra.
10	
11	### String
12	
13	A estrutura mais simples — uma chave aponta pra um valor.
14	
15	Serve pra qualquer coisa que caiba num único valor: texto, número, JSON serializado, binário.
16	
17	```
18	SET user:123:name "Dylan"
19	SET user:123:age  "25"
20	SET config:feature_flag "true"
21	```
22	
23	**Casos de uso:**
24	- Sessões de usuário
25	- Contadores (`INCR visits:page:home`)
26	- Feature flags
27	- Cache de qualquer coisa
28	
29	**Analogia:** uma gaveta com etiqueta. Você abre pelo nome e tem uma coisa dentro.
30	
31	---
32	
33	### List
34	
35	Uma coleção **ordenada** onde a ordem de inserção importa. Suporta inserção e remoção eficiente nas duas pontas.
36	
37	```
38	LPUSH fila:emails "email1"   ← insere no início
39	RPUSH fila:emails "email2"   ← insere no final
40	RPOP  fila:emails            ← remove do final (consome a fila)
41	```
42	
43	**Casos de uso:**
44	- Filas de jobs (LPUSH pra inserir, BRPOP pra consumir)
45	- Histórico de atividade (últimas 10 ações do usuário)
46	- Feed de notificações
47	
48	**Analogia:** uma fila de banco — quem chegou primeiro sai primeiro. Ou uma pilha de pratos — você empilha e desempilha pelo topo.
49	
50	---
51	
52	### Set
53	
54	Uma coleção de elementos **únicos sem ordem**. Duplicatas são ignoradas automaticamente.
55	
56	```
57	SADD tags:post:1 "redis" "backend" "cache"
58	SADD tags:post:1 "redis"   ← ignorado, já existe
59	
60	SMEMBERS tags:post:1       ← retorna todos
61	SISMEMBER tags:post:1 "redis"  ← verifica se existe
62	```
63	
64	Operações poderosas entre sets:
65	```
66	SUNION  set1 set2   ← união (tudo de ambos)
67	SINTER  set1 set2   ← interseção (só o que está nos dois)
68	SDIFF   set1 set2   ← diferença (o que está no set1 mas não no set2)
69	```
70	
71	**Casos de uso:**
72	- Tags de um post
73	- Usuários online
74	- Permissões de um usuário
75	- Amigos em comum (SINTER entre dois sets de amigos)
76	
77	**Analogia:** uma sacola onde você joga itens — mas se jogar o mesmo item duas vezes, só fica um.
78	
79	---
80	
81	### Sorted Set (ZSet)
82	
83	Como o Set, mas cada membro tem uma **pontuação (score)**. Os membros ficam sempre ordenados pela pontuação automaticamente.
84	
85	```
86	ZADD ranking:vendedores 1500 "Dylan"
87	ZADD ranking:vendedores 3200 "Alice"
88	ZADD ranking:vendedores 800  "Bob"
89	
90	ZRANGE ranking:vendedores 0 -1 WITHSCORES
91	→ Bob: 800
92	→ Dylan: 1500
93	→ Alice: 3200
94	```
95	
96	**Casos de uso:**
97	- Placar de jogos (leaderboard)
98	- Ranking de vendedores
99	- Fila de prioridade (score = prioridade)
100	- Rate limiting por tempo (score = timestamp)
101	
102	**Analogia:** uma competição com placar — você adiciona participantes com suas pontuações e o Redis mantém o ranking atualizado automaticamente.
103	
104	---
105	
106	### Hash
107	
108	Um mapa dentro de uma chave — em vez de um valor único, a chave guarda **múltiplos campos**.
109	
110	```
111	HSET user:123 name "Dylan" age "25" city "São Paulo"
112	
113	HGET    user:123 name       ← "Dylan"
114	HGETALL user:123            ← todos os campos
115	HDEL    user:123 city       ← remove um campo
116	```
117	
118	**Casos de uso:**
119	- Perfil de usuário (vários campos numa chave só)
120	- Dados de produto
121	- Configurações de sessão
122	
123	**Sem Hash** você precisaria de uma chave pra cada campo:
124	```
125	user:123:name  "Dylan"
126	user:123:age   "25"
127	user:123:city  "São Paulo"
128	```
129	
130	Com Hash, tudo numa chave só. Mais organizado e menos memória.
131	
132	**Analogia:** uma ficha de cadastro — tem campos diferentes (nome, idade, cidade) todos agrupados num único formulário.
133	
134	---
135	
136	### Stream
137	
138	Um log **append-only** — você só adiciona entradas, nunca edita ou remove. Cada entrada tem um ID com timestamp automático.
139	
140	```
141	XADD eventos:pagamentos * user_id 123 valor 150.00 status "aprovado"
142	XADD eventos:pagamentos * user_id 456 valor 89.90  status "recusado"
143	
144	XREAD COUNT 10 STREAMS eventos:pagamentos 0
145	→ retorna as últimas 10 entradas
146	```
147	
148	**Casos de uso:**
149	- Event sourcing (log de tudo que aconteceu)
150	- Fila de mensagens com múltiplos consumidores
151	- Auditoria de ações
152	- Integração entre serviços (parecido com Kafka, mas simples)
153	
154	**Analogia:** um livro de registro de portaria — você só escreve novas entradas, nunca apaga as antigas. Qualquer um pode ler o histórico completo.
155	
156	---
157	
158	### Resumo — Qual usar quando
159	
160	| Estrutura | Use quando |
161	|---|---|
162	| **String** | Um valor simples por chave — contador, sessão, flag |
163	| **List** | Ordem importa — fila, histórico, feed |
164	| **Set** | Unicidade importa — tags, permissões, amigos |
165	| **Sorted Set** | Unicidade + ordem por pontuação — ranking, placar |
166	| **Hash** | Múltiplos campos por chave — objeto, perfil |
167	| **Stream** | Log imutável — eventos, auditoria, mensageria |
168	
169	---
170	
171	## Study Order
172	
173	1. **String** — simple key/value
174	2. **TTL** — expiration
175	3. **Hash** — multiple fields per key
176	4. **List** — ordered sequences
177	5. **Set** — unique items
178	6. **Sorted Set** — ranking with scores
179	
180	---
181	
182	## String
183	
184	The simplest structure — a key maps directly to a value.
185	
186	**Go implementation:**
187	```go
188	map[string]string
189	```
190	
191	**Example:**
192	```go
193	store := map[string]string{}
194	store["name"] = "Dylan"
195	fmt.Println(store["name"]) // → "Dylan"
196	```
197	
198	**Cost:** O(1) for get and set.
199	
200	**Use when:** storing simple values — user sessions, config flags, counters.
201	
202	---
203	
204	## Hash
205	
206	A map inside a map. Each key holds multiple fields instead of a single value.
207	
208	**Go implementation:**
209	```go
210	map[string]map[string]string
211	```
212	
213	**Example:**
214	```go
215	store := map[string]map[string]string{}
216	store["user:1"] = map[string]string{
217	    "name": "Dylan",
218	    "age":  "25",
219	}
220	fmt.Println(store["user:1"]["name"]) // → "Dylan"
221	```
222	
223	**Cost:** O(1) for get and set.
224	
225	**Use when:** storing objects with multiple fields — user profiles, product data, session info.
226	
227	---
228	
229	## List
230	
231	An ordered sequence where insertion order is preserved. Supports efficient operations at both ends.
232	
233	**Go implementation:**
234	```go
235	[]string
236	```
237	
238	**Example:**
239	```go
240	list := []string{}
241	
242	// insert at end
243	list = append(list, "task1")
244	
245	// insert at start
246	list = append([]string{"task0"}, list...)
247	
248	// remove from start
249	first := list[0]
250	list = list[1:]
251	
252	// remove from end
253	last := list[len(list)-1]
254	list = list[:len(list)-1]
255	```
256	
257	**Cost:** O(1) at the ends, O(n) in the middle.
258	
259	**Use when:** queues, stacks, history logs, activity feeds.
260	
261	---
262	
263	## Set
264	
265	A collection of unique items — duplicates are ignored.
266	
267	**Go implementation:**
268	```go
269	map[string]struct{}
270	```
271	
272	> `struct{}` takes zero bytes — it's the idiomatic way to implement a set in Go.
273	
274	**Example:**
275	```go
276	set := map[string]struct{}{}
277	
278	// add
279	set["go"] = struct{}{}
280	set["go"] = struct{}{} // ignored — already exists
281	
282	// check existence
283	_, exists := set["go"] // → true
284	
285	// remove
286	delete(set, "go")
287	```
288	
289	**Cost:** O(1) for add, remove and lookup.
290	
291	**Use when:** unique visitors, tags, permissions, friend lists.
292	
293	---
294	
295	## Sorted Set
296	
297	Like a Set, but each item has a **score** that determines its position. Items are always kept in order by score.
298	
299	**Go implementation:**
300	Two structures working together:
301	```go
302	type SortedSet struct {
303	    scores  map[string]float64 // member → score (O(1) lookup)
304	    members []string           // kept sorted by score
305	}
306	```
307	
308	> A **skip list** or **heap** can be used internally for O(log n) inserts and lookups.
309	
310	**Example:**
311	```go
312	ss := SortedSet{
313	    scores:  map[string]float64{},
314	    members: []string{},
315	}
316	
317	// add member with score
318	ss.scores["Dylan"] = 100
319	ss.scores["Alice"] = 250
320	// members slice must be kept sorted after each insert
321	```
322	
323	**Cost:** O(log n) for insert and lookup.
324	
325	**Use when:** leaderboards, rankings, rate limiting, time-series data.
326	
327	---
328	
329	## TTL / Expiration
330	
331	A way to attach a time limit to any stored value — it automatically becomes invalid after the duration.
332	
333	**Go implementation:**
334	Two maps working together:
335	```go
336	data       map[string]string    // main store
337	expiration map[string]time.Time // key → expiry time
338	```
339	
340	**Example:**
341	```go
342	data := map[string]string{}
343	expiration := map[string]time.Time{}
344	
345	// store with TTL
346	data["session:abc"] = "token123"
347	expiration["session:abc"] = time.Now().Add(1 * time.Hour)
348	
349	// get with expiry check
350	func get(key string) (string, bool) {
351	    if exp, ok := expiration[key]; ok {
352	        if time.Now().After(exp) {
353	            delete(data, key)
354	            delete(expiration, key)
355	            return "", false // expired
356	        }
357	    }
358	    val, ok := data[key]
359	    return val, ok
360	}
361	```
362	
363	**Cost:** O(1) per access + periodic cleanup for expired keys.
364	
365	**Use when:** sessions, caches, rate limiters, temporary tokens.
366	
367	---
368	
369	## Complexity Summary
370	
371	| Structure | Get | Insert | Delete | Use case |
372	|-----------|-----|--------|--------|----------|
373	| String | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | Simple values |
374	| Hash | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | Objects with fields |
375	| List | O(n) | O(1) ends | O(1) ends | Queues, stacks |
376	| Set | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | Unique items |
377	| Sorted Set | O(log n) | O(log n) | O(log n) | Rankings |
378	| TTL | O(1) | O(1) | O(1) | Expiring keys |
379

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Expiration / TTL
2	
3	Expiration (TTL) is the ability to set a time limit on a key. After the TTL, the key is removed automatically.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Key Concepts
8	
9	- **TTL (Time To Live):** how long a key should exist.
10	- **Expire time:** absolute timestamp when the key becomes invalid.
11	- **Expired key:** a key whose TTL has passed.
12	
13	---
14	
15	## Example (10s TTL, read at 12s)
16	
17	- Key is set with TTL = 10 seconds.
18	- If the client reads the key after 12 seconds, it is already expired.
19	- The server deletes it and returns "missing".
20	
21	---
22	
23	## Lazy Expiration
24	
25	**Lazy expire** means keys are removed only when they are accessed.
26	
27	**How it works:**
28	- Client requests a key
29	- Server checks if TTL has expired
30	- If expired, delete and return missing
31	
32	**Pros:**
33	- Very cheap and simple
34	- No background work
35	
36	**Cons:**
37	- Expired keys can stay in memory if never accessed
38	
39	---
40	
41	## Active Expiration
42	
43	**Active expire** means the server periodically scans keys and removes expired ones.
44	
45	**How it works:**
46	- Timer runs every N milliseconds
47	- Random sample of keys is checked
48	- Expired keys are deleted
49	
50	**Pros:**
51	- Frees memory even if keys are never accessed
52	
53	**Cons:**
54	- Background cost (CPU)
55	- Needs tuning to avoid latency spikes
56	
57	---
58	
59	## Hybrid Approach
60	
61	A common strategy is to use **both**:
62	- Lazy expire on access
63	- Active expire in background
64	
65	This balances correctness and performance.
66	
67	---
68	
69	## Eviction Policies
70	
71	Eviction happens **when memory is full**, not just when keys expire.
72	
73	Common policies:
74	
75	- **noeviction**
76	  - Reject new writes when memory is full
77	
78	- **allkeys-lru**
79	  - Remove least recently used key (any key)
80	
81	- **allkeys-lfu**
82	  - Remove least frequently used key (any key)
83	
84	- **allkeys-random**
85	  - Remove random key
86	
87	- **volatile-lru**
88	  - Remove least recently used key **with TTL**
89	
90	- **volatile-lfu**
91	  - Remove least frequently used key **with TTL**
92	
93	- **volatile-random**
94	  - Remove random key **with TTL**
95	
96	- **volatile-ttl**
97	  - Remove key with the **shortest remaining TTL**
98	
99	---
100	
101	## Implementation Model (Concept)
102	
103	You can store TTLs in a separate map:
104	
105	```
106	store:   map[string]string
107	expires: map[string]time.Time
108	```
109	
110	Check on read:
111	- If key has TTL and it expired -> delete and return missing
112	
113	Background cleanup:
114	- On a timer, scan a sample of keys and remove expired
115	
116	---
117	
118	## Quick Notes
119	
120	- TTL is not a promise of exact time, only an upper bound
121	- Lazy expire ensures correctness on access
122	- Active expire ensures memory does not fill with dead keys
123	
124	---
125	
126	## Terms to Remember
127	
128	- TTL
129	- Expire time
130	- Lazy expiration
131	- Active expiration
132	- Eviction policy
133	- LRU / LFU
134

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Kubernetes
2	
3	Kubernetes (K8s) is a container orchestration platform — it manages where and how your containers run across multiple machines.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## The Problem It Solves
8	
9	Docker lets you run a container on one machine. But in production you have questions Docker alone cannot answer:
10	
11	- What if that machine dies?
12	- How do I run 10 copies of my backend?
13	- How do I update my app without downtime?
14	- How do containers talk to each other?
15	
16	Kubernetes answers all of these.
17	
18	**Analogy:** Docker is a shipping container. Kubernetes is the port that decides which ship carries which container, handles damaged ships, and keeps everything moving.
19	
20	---
21	
22	## Core Concepts
23	
24	### Cluster
25	
26	A **cluster** is the full Kubernetes environment — a set of machines working together.
27	
28	```
29	Cluster
30	├── Control Plane (the brain)
31	└── Nodes (the workers)
32	```
33	
34	### Node
35	
36	A **node** is a machine (physical or virtual) that runs your containers. You can have many nodes in a cluster.
37	
38	### Pod
39	
40	A **pod** is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes — a wrapper around one or more containers.
41	
42	```
43	Pod
44	└── Container (your app)
45	```
46	
47	- Pods are ephemeral — they can die and be replaced at any time
48	- Each pod gets its own IP address inside the cluster
49	- If a pod dies, Kubernetes creates a new one automatically
50	
51	### Deployment
52	
53	A **deployment** tells Kubernetes how many copies (replicas) of your pod to keep running at all times.
54	
55	```yaml
56	replicas: 3  ← always keep 3 pods running
57	```
58	
59	If one pod dies, the deployment notices and creates a new one. You declare the desired state — Kubernetes figures out how to get there.
60	
61	### Service
62	
63	A **service** is a stable network endpoint that sits in front of your pods.
64	
65	Since pods come and go (new IPs each time), a service gives you a fixed address that always routes to healthy pods.
66	
67	```
68	Client → Service (fixed IP) → Pod 1
69	                            → Pod 2
70	                            → Pod 3
71	```
72	
73	**Analogy:** the service is the receptionist — you always call the same number, and she routes you to whoever is available.
74	
75	### Ingress
76	
77	An **ingress** exposes your services to the outside world (internet).
78	
79	```
80	Internet → Ingress → Service → Pods
81	```
82	
83	It handles routing, TLS termination, and domain-based routing (e.g. `api.myapp.com` → backend service).
84	
85	---
86	
87	## How It Connects to What You Already Know
88	
89	You studied horizontal scaling — adding more backend instances when under load. Kubernetes is what manages that in production:
90	
91	```
92	High traffic detected
93	→ Kubernetes scales Deployment from 3 to 10 pods automatically
94	→ Service routes traffic across all 10
95	→ Traffic drops → scales back to 3
96	```
97	
98	This is called **autoscaling**.
99	
100	---
101	
102	## Desired State vs Actual State
103	
104	This is the core philosophy of Kubernetes:
105	
106	You describe **what you want** (desired state), Kubernetes figures out **how to make it happen** (actual state).
107	
108	```
109	You say:   "I want 3 replicas of my backend running"
110	K8s does:  creates pods, monitors them, replaces dead ones
111	```
112	
113	You never say "start this container on that machine" — you declare intent and Kubernetes acts.
114	
115	---
116	
117	## Key Objects Summary
118	
119	| Object | What it does |
120	|---|---|
121	| Pod | Runs your container |
122	| Deployment | Manages how many pods run and keeps them healthy |
123	| Service | Stable network access to pods |
124	| Ingress | Exposes services to the internet |
125	| ConfigMap | Stores configuration (non-sensitive) |
126	| Secret | Stores sensitive data (passwords, tokens) |
127	| Namespace | Logical isolation within a cluster |
128	
129	---
130	
131	## Rolling Updates — Zero Downtime Deploys
132	
133	When you deploy a new version of your app, Kubernetes updates pods one at a time:
134	
135	```
136	v1 v1 v1 v1
137	→ replace one at a time
138	v2 v1 v1 v1
139	v2 v2 v1 v1
140	v2 v2 v2 v1
141	v2 v2 v2 v2
142	```
143	
144	At no point is the app fully down. If the new version is broken, Kubernetes rolls back automatically.
145	
146	---
147	
148	## Kubernetes and Redis
149	
150	In your MiniRedisGo context:
151	
152	```
153	[Backend Pods x3]  →  Redis Service  →  Redis Pod (primary)
154	                                      →  Redis Pod (replica)
155	```
156	
157	- Your backend scales horizontally across pods
158	- Redis runs as a stateful workload with replication
159	- The Service ensures backends always find Redis at the same address
160	
161	Redis in Kubernetes typically uses a **StatefulSet** instead of a Deployment — because Redis pods need stable identities and persistent storage, unlike stateless backends.
162	
163	---
164	
165	## Design Notes
166	
167	- Kubernetes does not build your containers — that is Docker's job
168	- K8s manages state at the infrastructure level, not the application level
169	- `kubectl` is the CLI to interact with a cluster
170	- Kubernetes is complex — managed versions (GKE, EKS, AKS) hide most of the operational burden
171	
172	---
173	
174	## Common kubectl Commands
175	
176	```bash
177	kubectl get pods                    # list running pods
178	kubectl get deployments             # list deployments
179	kubectl describe pod <name>         # inspect a pod
180	kubectl logs <pod-name>             # view pod logs
181	kubectl scale deployment app --replicas=5  # scale manually
182	kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml    # apply a config file
183	```
184	
185	---
186	
187	## Related Notes
188	
189	- [[Replication]]
190	- [[SecurityAuth]]
191	- [[PubSub]]
192

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Memory Management
2	
3	Memory management is about understanding **where data lives, how it gets there, and how it gets cleaned up**.
4	
5	Every program needs memory to store data while it runs. How that memory is requested, used, and released determines the performance and reliability of the system.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Stack vs Heap
10	
11	Every program has two main places to store data:
12	
13	```
14	┌─────────────────────────────────┐
15	│             STACK               │  ← fast, small, automatic
16	├─────────────────────────────────┤
17	│              HEAP               │  ← slower, large, managed
18	└─────────────────────────────────┘
19	```
20	
21	---
22	
23	## Stack
24	
25	The stack is fast, automatic memory tied to function calls.
26	
27	- When a function is called → a stack frame is created
28	- When a function returns → the stack frame is destroyed
29	- No allocator involved — just a pointer moving up and down
30	- Each thread has its own stack
31	
32	```
33	main() calls handleRequest()
34	  handleRequest() calls parseCommand()
35	
36	Stack:
37	┌──────────────────┐
38	│  parseCommand()  │  ← top (current function)
39	├──────────────────┤
40	│ handleRequest()  │
41	├──────────────────┤
42	│     main()       │
43	└──────────────────┘
44	
45	parseCommand() returns → frame destroyed
46	handleRequest() returns → frame destroyed
47	```
48	
49	### What lives on the stack
50	
51	- Local variables with known, fixed size
52	- Function arguments and return values
53	- Variables that do not outlive the function
54	
55	**Analogy:** a whiteboard you erase after each meeting. Instant to write, instant to erase, but limited space.
56	
57	---
58	
59	## Heap
60	
61	The heap is a large pool of memory for data that needs to **outlive a function** or has a **size unknown at compile time**.
62	
63	- Slower to allocate — goes through a memory allocator (jemalloc, tcmalloc, etc.)
64	- Lives until explicitly freed or garbage collected
65	- Shared across threads
66	
67	If a variable needs to survive after the function that created it returns, it must live on the heap.
68	
69	**Analogy:** a storage unit you rent. More space, but someone has to manage it — and the rent has to be paid until you leave.
70	
71	---
72	
73	## Manual vs Automatic Memory Management
74	
75	Different languages handle heap cleanup differently:
76	
77	| Approach | Language | How |
78	|---|---|---|
79	| **Manual** | C, C++ | Programmer calls `free()` — fast but error-prone |
80	| **Garbage Collected** | Go, Java, Python | Runtime cleans up automatically |
81	| **Ownership system** | Rust | Compiler enforces rules at compile time — no GC needed |
82	
83	### Manual (C / C++)
84	
85	```c
86	char *buf = malloc(1024);  // allocate
87	// use buf...
88	free(buf);                 // you must free it
89	// if you forget → memory leak
90	// if you free twice → crash
91	// if you use after free → undefined behavior
92	```
93	
94	Fast, but dangerous. Redis is written in C — every allocation is manual.
95	
96	### Garbage Collector
97	
98	The runtime periodically scans the heap, finds data that nothing points to anymore, and frees it automatically.
99	
100	```
101	heap:
102	[User A ✓][string ✓][int ✗][User B ✓][[]byte ✗]
103	                      ↑ nothing points here    ↑ nothing points here
104	
105	GC runs → frees the ✗ blocks
106	```
107	
108	Safer and easier, but the GC has a cost — it runs on CPU and can cause latency pauses.
109	
110	### Ownership (Rust)
111	
112	The compiler tracks who owns each piece of memory. When the owner goes out of scope, memory is freed automatically — at compile time, with no runtime cost.
113	
114	```rust
115	{
116	    let s = String::from("hello");  // s owns the memory
117	    // use s...
118	}  // s goes out of scope → memory freed automatically, no GC needed
119	```
120	
121	---
122	
123	## Garbage Collector — How It Works
124	
125	The most common GC algorithm is **Mark and Sweep**:
126	
127	**Phase 1 — Mark:**
128	Starting from roots (stack variables, globals), follow all pointers and mark everything reachable:
129	
130	```
131	roots → User{} ✓ → name string ✓
132	      → cache map ✓ → entries ✓
133	      → (old buffer nobody points to) ✗ — never reached
134	```
135	
136	**Phase 2 — Sweep:**
137	Free everything unmarked:
138	
139	```
140	[User ✓][string ✓][buffer ✗ freed][map ✓][[]byte ✗ freed]
141	```
142	
143	### Stop-the-World vs Concurrent GC
144	
145	Older GC implementations paused the entire program while collecting:
146	
147	```
148	Stop-the-world:
149	────────────────|░░░░░░░GC░░░░░░|────────────────
150	program runs     everything pauses   program resumes
151	```
152	
153	This caused latency spikes. Modern GCs (Go, JVM G1) run **concurrently** — most work happens while the program keeps running, with only brief pauses.
154	
155	---
156	
157	## Memory Leaks
158	
159	A memory leak happens when memory is allocated but never freed — the program slowly consumes more and more RAM until it crashes or is killed.
160	
161	Common causes:
162	- **Manual:** forgetting to call `free()`
163	- **GC languages:** keeping a reference to data you no longer need (GC can't collect it if something still points to it)
164	- **Caches without eviction:** storing data forever without a TTL
165	
166	```
167	// leak in a GC language — cache grows forever
168	var cache = map[string][]byte{}
169	
170	func store(key string, data []byte) {
171	    cache[key] = data  // never removed → GC never collects it
172	}
173	```
174	
175	---
176	
177	## Stack vs Heap — Summary
178	
179	| | **Stack** | **Heap** |
180	|---|---|---|
181	| Speed | Instant | Slower (allocator + GC) |
182	| Size | Small, per-thread | Large, shared |
183	| Lifetime | Function scope | Until freed or GC'd |
184	| Managed by | Automatically | Allocator + GC or programmer |
185	| Fragmentation | None | Yes |
186	| GC involvement | None | Yes |
187	
188	---
189	
190	## Design Notes
191	
192	- Stack allocations are essentially free — prefer them when possible
193	- Heap allocations have cost: allocator time + future cleanup work
194	- GC is not free — it runs on CPU and competes with your program
195	- Memory leaks in long-running servers are fatal — they compound over time
196	- Measure before optimizing — profilers show exactly where allocations happen
197	
198	---
199	
200	## Language-Specific Notes
201	
202	- **Go** → see [[GO/MemoryManagement]]
203	- **Redis (C)** → manual memory management via jemalloc, see [[Performance]]
204	
205	---
206	
207	## Related Notes
208	
209	- [[Performance]]
210	- [[Concurrency]]
211	- [[Persistence]]
212

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Performance
2	
3	Performance is about understanding **where time and memory go** — and reducing both.
4	
5	Three pillars: **latency**, **memory allocation**, and **benchmarking**.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Latency
10	
11	Latency is the time between a request being sent and a response being received.
12	
13	```
14	Client sends request  →  [time passes]  →  Client receives response
15	                              ↑
16	                          latency
17	```
18	
19	**Analogy:** you order food at a restaurant. Latency is the time between ordering and the food arriving at your table.
20	
21	### Latency vs Throughput
22	
23	These are related but different:
24	
25	|          | **Latency**                       | **Throughput**                |
26	| -------- | --------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
27	| Question | How fast is one request?          | How many requests per second? |
28	| Unit     | milliseconds (ms)                 | requests/sec (RPS)            |
29	| Analogy  | How fast one car crosses a bridge | How many cars cross per hour  |
30	
31	You can have high throughput with high latency — many requests processed, but each one slowly. The goal is usually both: fast and many.
32	
33	### Sources of Latency
34	
35	```
36	Total latency = network + queue + processing + response
37	
38	Network      → physical distance, hops between servers
39	Queue        → waiting behind other requests
40	Processing   → actual computation time
41	Response     → sending the result back
42	```
43	
44	### Latency Numbers Worth Knowing
45	
46	| Operation                 | Approximate Latency |
47	| ------------------------- | ------------------- |
48	| L1 cache access           | ~1 ns               |
49	| RAM access                | ~100 ns             |
50	| SSD read                  | ~100 µs             |
51	| Network (same datacenter) | ~1 ms               |
52	| Network (cross-continent) | ~100 ms             |
53	| HDD read                  | ~10 ms              |
54	
55	This is why Redis is fast — it reads from RAM (~100 ns), not disk.
56	
57	### P50, P95, P99 — Percentile Latency
58	
59	Averages lie. A system with average 10ms latency might have 1% of requests taking 2 seconds.
60	
61	Percentiles tell the real story:
62	
63	```
64	P50  = 50% of requests finish in under X ms  (median)
65	P95  = 95% of requests finish in under X ms
66	P99  = 99% of requests finish in under X ms
67	P999 = 99.9% of requests finish in under X ms
68	```
69	
70	**Analogy:** average salary in a company with one billionaire CEO looks high. The median (P50) tells you what most employees actually earn.
71	
72	In production, you optimize P99 — the worst 1% still affects real users.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## Memory Allocator
77	
78	A memory allocator manages how your program requests and releases memory from the OS.
79	
80	Your program never talks directly to hardware. When you need memory, you talk to the allocator, and it talks to the OS:
81	
82	```
83	Your code  →  malloc(64)  →  Allocator  →  OS (only when necessary)
84	```
85	
86	The OS works in pages (usually 4KB). Allocating 4KB every time you need 64 bytes would be wasteful. The allocator requests large pages from the OS and subdivides them internally.
87	
88	### How Memory Allocation Works
89	
90	When your program needs memory:
91	
92	```
93	Program  →  "I need 64 bytes"  →  Allocator
94	Allocator → finds free block → returns pointer
95	Program uses memory
96	Program  →  "I'm done with this"  →  Allocator
97	Allocator → marks block as free → available for reuse
98	```
99	
100	### The Problem: Fragmentation
101	
102	Over time, memory ends up looking like swiss cheese — lots of small free holes that are hard to reuse:
103	
104	```
105	Before:
106	[████████████████████████████████] 100MB contiguous
107	
108	After many allocs/frees:
109	[used][    ][used][used][    ][used][    ]
110	       16MB        8MB        4MB
111	```
112	
113	You have 28MB free total, but in separate chunks. If you need 20MB contiguous — it is not available.
114	
115	### How ptmalloc (glibc) Works — and Why It Is Slow
116	
117	ptmalloc was designed in 1987 for single-threaded workloads and had threading bolted on later. Its model is too simple for modern servers:
118	
119	```
120	Thread 1 wants to alloc  →  locks the heap  →  allocs  →  unlocks
121	Thread 2 wants to alloc  →  waiting in queue...
122	Thread 3 wants to alloc  →  waiting in queue...
123	```
124	
125	In systems with many threads, threads sleep waiting for the lock. This becomes latency.
126	
127	### How jemalloc Solves This — Arenas
128	
129	jemalloc divides memory into independent **arenas**. Each thread is assigned to an arena, so threads rarely compete for the same lock:
130	
131	```
132	Thread 1  →  Arena 1  (own lock)
133	Thread 2  →  Arena 2  (own lock)
134	Thread 3  →  Arena 1  (shares with Thread 1, but rarely conflicts)
135	```
136	
137	Inside each arena, jemalloc divides allocations into **size classes** — predefined fixed sizes:
138	
139	```
140	Small  (≤ 14KB)   →  thread-local cache (tcache) — zero locks
141	Large  (14KB–4MB) →  directly from arena
142	Huge   (> 4MB)    →  mmap'd directly from OS
143	```
144	
145	For small allocations (the majority), jemalloc uses a **per-thread cache** (tcache) — zero contention, zero lock.
146	
147	jemalloc also solves fragmentation with size classes — instead of allocating exactly what was requested, it rounds up to the nearest fixed size:
148	
149	```
150	You request 57 bytes  →  jemalloc allocates 64 bytes (next size class)
151	You request 100 bytes →  jemalloc allocates 128 bytes
152	```
153	
154	Result: blocks of the same size stay together, easy to reuse:
155	
156	```
157	[64][64][64][64][64]  ← all equal, any one works for any ≤64 byte request
158	```
159	
160	### Common Allocators
161	
162	| Allocator | Used by | Notes |
163	|---|---|---|
164	| **glibc (ptmalloc)** | Default on Linux | General purpose, slow under contention |
165	| **jemalloc** | Redis, Firefox, Rust | Best fragmentation handling for long-running servers |
166	| **tcmalloc** | Google, Go | Per-thread private cache, best throughput for high-concurrency |
167	| **mimalloc** | Microsoft | Modern, very fast across workloads |
168	
169	### jemalloc vs tcmalloc
170	
171	| | **jemalloc** | **tcmalloc** |
172	|---|---|---|
173	| Strong at | Fragmentation — long-running servers | Throughput — many threads, many small allocs |
174	| Thread model | Shared arenas | Fully private per-thread cache |
175	| Ideal for | Redis, databases | HTTP servers, Google-style applications |
176	| Weak at | High-frequency small allocs | Long-running with varied allocation patterns |
177	
178	### How This Appears in Redis
179	
180	Redis reports two memory metrics:
181	
182	```bash
183	INFO memory
184	
185	used_memory:     1000000000   # 1GB — what Redis thinks it is using
186	used_memory_rss: 1500000000   # 1.5GB — what the OS sees
187	
188	mem_fragmentation_ratio: 1.5  # used_by_OS / used_by_Redis
189	```
190	
191	**Fragmentation ratio above 1.5 = problem.** jemalloc is holding memory in its internal bins that the OS counts as used, but Redis is not actively using.
192	
193	**How to fix:**
194	
195	```bash
196	MEMORY PURGE  # Redis 4.0+ — forces jemalloc to return idle pages to the OS
197	```
198	
199	Redis 4.0+ also has **active defragmentation** — automatically reallocates fragmented keys in the background.
200	
201	### Why Redis Chose jemalloc
202	
203	Redis is a process that runs for weeks or months, making millions of small allocations (keys, values, internal structures). This is exactly the scenario where jemalloc excels — controlled fragmentation over time.
204	
205	With ptmalloc, after weeks of running the process would consume 3-4x more RAM than the actual data occupies.
206	
207	### Why It Matters
208	
209	A bad allocator on a high-traffic system can:
210	- Fragment memory → process uses 2GB RAM but only 1GB is actual data
211	- Slow down allocation → extra latency on every operation
212	- Cause unexpected OOM (out of memory) kills
213	
214	**References:**
215	- [Memory Allocators: malloc vs. tcmalloc vs. jemalloc](https://howtech.substack.com/p/memory-allocators-malloc-vs-tcmalloc)
216	- [Exploring Different Memory Allocators](https://dev.to/frosnerd/libmalloc-jemalloc-tcmalloc-mimalloc-exploring-different-memory-allocators-4lp3)
217	- [How Redis jemalloc Memory Allocator Works](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-03-31-redis-jemalloc-memory-allocator/view)
218	- [How to Troubleshoot Redis Memory Fragmentation](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-03-31-redis-how-to-troubleshoot-redis-memory-fragmentation/view)
219	- [Testing Memory Allocators: ptmalloc2 vs tcmalloc vs hoard vs jemalloc](http://ithare.com/testing-memory-allocators-ptmalloc2-tcmalloc-hoard-jemalloc-while-trying-to-simulate-real-world-loads/)
220	
221	---
222	
223	## Benchmarking
224	
225	Benchmarking is the process of **measuring performance under controlled conditions** to understand where your system stands and where it can improve.
226	
227	### Why Benchmark
228	
229	- Verify your system meets performance requirements
230	- Compare two implementations (which is faster?)
231	- Catch regressions — did the new code make things slower?
232	- Find bottlenecks — where is time actually being spent?
233	
234	### Types of Benchmarks
235	
236	**Microbenchmark** — measures a single small operation in isolation
237	
238	```go
239	// how fast is a single SET command?
240	for i := 0; i < 1000000; i++ {
241	    redis.Set("key", "value")
242	}
243	```
244	
245	**Load test** — simulates real traffic at scale
246	
247	```
248	Send 10,000 requests/sec for 60 seconds
249	Measure: latency P50/P95/P99, error rate, throughput
250	```
251	
252	**Stress test** — pushes beyond expected limits to find breaking points
253	
254	```
255	Keep increasing load until the system fails
256	Observe: when does it degrade? when does it break?
257	```
258	
259	### Common Benchmarking Tools
260	
261	| Tool | Use case |
262	|---|---|
263	| **redis-benchmark** | Built-in Redis benchmark |
264	| **wrk / wrk2** | HTTP load testing |
265	| **k6** | Scriptable load testing |
266	| **pprof** (Go) | CPU and memory profiling |
267	| **perf** (Linux) | System-level performance analysis |
268	
269	### Redis Benchmark Example
270	
271	```bash
272	# run 100,000 SET commands with 50 concurrent clients
273	redis-benchmark -t set -n 100000 -c 50
274	
275	# output:
276	# 100000 requests completed in 1.23 seconds
277	# 50 parallel clients
278	# Latency: avg 0.5ms, P99 1.2ms
279	```
280	
281	### Benchmarking Pitfalls
282	
283	- **Benchmark in production-like conditions** — your laptop is not your server
284	- **Warm up first** — cold start skews results (JIT, caches, connections)
285	- **Measure P99, not just average** — averages hide outliers
286	- **Isolate variables** — change one thing at a time
287	- **Run multiple times** — variance is real, one run is not enough
288	
289	---
290	
291	## How They Connect
292	
293	```
294	Latency       →  what you measure (how slow is it?)
295	Allocator     →  one source of latency (memory operations)
296	Benchmarking  →  how you find where latency comes from
297	```
298	
299	The loop:
300	
301	```
302	Benchmark → find bottleneck → fix it (tune allocator, reduce copies, cache) → benchmark again
303	```
304	
305	---
306	
307	## In Redis Context
308	
309	- Redis is fast because it operates entirely in **RAM** — latency in microseconds
310	- Uses **jemalloc** to minimize fragmentation over long uptimes
311	- **redis-benchmark** is built-in for quick performance checks
312	- Latency spikes in Redis often come from: large keys, blocking commands, persistence flushes (AOF fsync)
313	
314	---
315	
316	## Design Notes
317	
318	- Premature optimization is the root of all evil — benchmark first, optimize what the data shows
319	- Latency budget: know how much latency each layer is allowed to add
320	- Memory fragmentation ratio above 1.5 in Redis is a warning sign
321	- Always measure before and after an optimization to confirm it helped
322	
323	---
324	
325	## Related Notes
326	
327	- [[Replication]]
328	- [[Persistence]]
329	- [[TLS]]
330

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Persistence
2	
3	Persistence is the ability to keep data alive after the process stops. Without it, everything stored in memory is lost when the server restarts.
4	
5	There are two main strategies:
6	
7	---
8	
9	## RDB — Snapshot
10	
11	RDB (Redis Database) takes a **full snapshot** of all data in memory and saves it to disk at a given point in time.
12	
13	```
14	memory ──► [snapshot] ──► dump.rdb (file on disk)
15	```
16	
17	**How it works:**
18	- At a defined interval (or manually), Go forks the process
19	- The child process writes a full copy of the data to a `.rdb` file
20	- The main process keeps running without interruption
21	
22	**Configuration example:**
23	```
24	save 900 1     # snapshot if at least 1 key changed in 900s
25	save 300 10    # snapshot if at least 10 keys changed in 300s
26	save 60 10000  # snapshot if at least 10000 keys changed in 60s
27	```
28	
29	**Pros:**
30	- Compact single file — easy to backup and transfer
31	- Fast restarts — loading one file is quick
32	- No performance impact during normal operation
33	
34	**Cons:**
35	- Data between snapshots is lost on crash
36	- If the server crashes at 4:59 and snapshot runs every 5 minutes, you lose ~5 minutes of data
37	
38	**Use when:** you can tolerate some data loss and want fast restarts.
39	
40	---
41	
42	## AOF — Append-Only File
43	
44	AOF records **every write operation** as it happens, appending it to a log file. On restart, it replays the log to rebuild the state.
45	
46	```
47	SET name Dylan  ──► appended to file
48	SET age 25      ──► appended to file
49	DEL name        ──► appended to file
50	
51	restart ──► replay all entries ──► state restored
52	```
53	
54	**How it works:**
55	- Every write command is appended to `appendonly.aof`
56	- On restart, the file is read line by line and each command is re-executed
57	- Over time the file grows — a rewrite (compaction) can shrink it
58	
59	**Fsync policies:**
60	```
61	appendfsync always    # write to disk on every command (safest, slowest)
62	appendfsync everysec  # write to disk every second (good balance)
63	appendfsync no        # let the OS decide (fastest, least safe)
64	```
65	
66	**Pros:**
67	- At most 1 second of data loss (with `everysec`)
68	- Human-readable log — you can inspect or edit it
69	- More durable than RDB
70	
71	**Cons:**
72	- Larger file size than RDB
73	- Slower restarts — must replay every command
74	- Rewrite process needed to keep file size manageable
75	
76	**Use when:** data durability is critical and you cannot afford to lose more than 1 second of data.
77	
78	---
79	
80	## RDB vs AOF
81	
82	| | RDB | AOF |
83	|---|---|---|
84	| What it saves | Full snapshot | Every write command |
85	| Data loss risk | Minutes (between snapshots) | At most 1 second |
86	| File size | Small | Large (grows over time) |
87	| Restart speed | Fast | Slow (replays all commands) |
88	| Best for | Backups, fast restarts | Durability, audit log |
89	
90	---
91	
92	## Using Both Together
93	
94	RDB and AOF can be used simultaneously — RDB for fast restarts and AOF for durability. On restart, AOF takes priority since it has more recent data.
95	
96	```
97	RDB  ──► fast restore of base snapshot
98	AOF  ──► replay only the commands since last snapshot
99	```
100	
101	---
102	
103	## No Persistence
104	
105	If neither is enabled, the data lives only in memory — everything is lost on restart. Useful for pure caching where data can be rebuilt from the source.
106	
107	```
108	restart ──► empty state
109	```
110	
111	---
112	
113	## Implementation Concept in Go
114	
115	```go
116	// RDB — save snapshot
117	func saveRDB(store map[string]string) error {
118	    data, _ := json.Marshal(store)
119	    return os.WriteFile("dump.rdb", data, 0644)
120	}
121	
122	// RDB — load snapshot
123	func loadRDB() map[string]string {
124	    data, err := os.ReadFile("dump.rdb")
125	    if err != nil {
126	        return map[string]string{}
127	    }
128	    store := map[string]string{}
129	    json.Unmarshal(data, &store)
130	    return store
131	}
132	
133	// AOF — append command
134	func appendAOF(command string) error {
135	    f, err := os.OpenFile("appendonly.aof", os.O_APPEND|os.O_CREATE|os.O_WRONLY, 0644)
136	    if err != nil {
137	        return err
138	    }
139	    defer f.Close()
140	    _, err = f.WriteString(command + "\n")
141	    return err
142	}
143	```
144

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Pub/Sub
2	
3	Pub/Sub (Publish/Subscribe) is a messaging pattern where senders (**publishers**) do not send messages directly to receivers. Instead, they publish messages to a **channel**, and any interested party (**subscriber**) receives them.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Core Terms
8	
9	- **Publisher:** the one who sends a message to a channel.
10	- **Subscriber:** the one who listens to a channel and receives messages.
11	- **Channel:** the named pipe through which messages flow.
12	- **Message:** the data published to a channel.
13	- **Fanout:** when one message is delivered to multiple subscribers at once.
14	- **Broker:** the middleman that manages channels and routes messages (e.g. Redis, Kafka).
15	
16	---
17	
18	## How it Works
19	
20	```
21	Publisher → [channel: "orders"] → Subscriber A
22	                                → Subscriber B
23	                                → Subscriber C
24	```
25	
26	1. Subscriber A, B, and C subscribe to the channel `"orders"`
27	2. Publisher sends a message to `"orders"`
28	3. All three subscribers receive the message simultaneously
29	
30	The publisher does not know who is listening — it just publishes.
31	The subscribers do not know who is publishing — they just listen.
32	
33	---
34	
35	## Fanout
36	
37	Fanout is when **one message reaches multiple subscribers** at the same time.
38	
39	This is the default behavior in Pub/Sub — every subscriber on the channel gets a copy of the message.
40	
41	```
42	1 message published → N subscribers receive it
43	```
44	
45	Fanout is useful when multiple parts of your system need to react to the same event independently.
46	
47	---
48	
49	## Pub/Sub vs Direct Messaging
50	
51	| | **Direct (REST/queue)** | **Pub/Sub** |
52	|---|---|---|
53	| Sender knows receiver? | Yes | No |
54	| One message → many receivers? | No | Yes |
55	| Coupling | Tight | Loose |
56	| Example | HTTP request | Event notification |
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Practical Examples
61	
62	- **Order placed** → notify inventory service, billing service, and email service at the same time
63	- **User signed up** → trigger welcome email, analytics event, and onboarding flow independently
64	- **Price updated** → all connected clients receive the new price in real time
65	
66	---
67	
68	## Pub/Sub in Redis
69	
70	Redis has native Pub/Sub support:
71	
72	```
73	# Subscriber listens to a channel
74	SUBSCRIBE orders
75	
76	# Publisher sends a message
77	PUBLISH orders "new order: #123"
78	```
79	
80	Redis delivers the message to all active subscribers instantly.
81	
82	**Important:** Redis Pub/Sub is **fire and forget** — if a subscriber is offline when the message is published, it misses it. There is no persistence, no backlog.
83	
84	## How Applications Read from Redis
85	
86	There are two different models — do not confuse them:
87	
88	**Normal read (cache/GET)** — the application actively queries Redis:
89	
90	```
91	[Backend]  →  GET user:123
92	[Redis]    →  returns the value
93	```
94	
95	The application asks, Redis answers. The application controls when to fetch.
96	
97	**Pub/Sub** — the application stays connected and Redis pushes messages to it:
98	
99	```
100	[Backend]  →  SUBSCRIBE orders
101	              (stays connected, waiting)
102	
103	[Redis]    →  message arrives → pushes to backend
104	```
105	
106	The application does not ask repeatedly — it just listens and reacts when something arrives.
107	
108	| | **Normal read** | **Pub/Sub** |
109	|---|---|---|
110	| Who initiates | Application | Redis |
111	| Model | Pull | Push |
112	| When to use | Fetch a specific value | React to events in real time |
113	
114	> In cache you go fetch the data. In Pub/Sub the data comes to you when it happens.
115	
116	## Pub/Sub vs Replication
117	
118	These are completely separate mechanisms in Redis — a common source of confusion:
119	
120	| | **Replication** | **Pub/Sub** |
121	|---|---|---|
122	| Purpose | Sync data between Redis nodes | Dispatch messages between your services |
123	| Who uses it | Redis internally | Your application |
124	| What travels | Write commands | Custom messages |
125	| Subscriber is | A Redis replica | Your backend/service |
126	
127	Replication is internal to Redis — the primary streams commands to replicas automatically.
128	Pub/Sub is for your application — your services publish and subscribe to communicate with each other.
129	
130	```
131	[Order Service]  →  PUBLISH orders "new order #123"
132	                           ↓
133	                     Redis (broker)
134	                           ↓
135	          ┌────────────────┼────────────────┐
136	   [Inventory]         [Billing]          [Email]
137	```
138	
139	Redis acts as the **messenger** between your services — not between its own replicas.
140	
141	---
142	
143	## Fire and Forget — The Main Limitation
144	
145	Unlike replication (which has a backlog and can catch up), Pub/Sub in Redis does not store messages.
146	
147	- Subscriber online → receives the message ✓
148	- Subscriber offline → message is lost ✗
149	
150	If you need guaranteed delivery, you need a different tool (e.g. Redis Streams, Kafka).
151	
152	---
153	
154	## Design Notes
155	
156	- Pub/Sub decouples producers from consumers — they do not need to know about each other
157	- Great for real-time notifications, live updates, and event broadcasting
158	- Not suitable when message delivery must be guaranteed
159	- Channel names are just strings — no schema, no enforced structure
160	
161	---
162	
163	## Related Notes
164	
165	- [[Replication]]
166	- [[Persistence]]
167

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Redis
2	
3	Redis (Remote Dictionary Server) is an in-memory data structure store used as a database, cache, message broker, and streaming engine.
4	
5	It defines:
6	- key-value storage with sub-millisecond latency
7	- rich data structures beyond simple strings
8	- persistence options for durability
9	- built-in replication and clustering
10	
11	---
12	
13	## Why Redis?
14	
15	- Extremely fast — all operations happen in memory
16	- Versatile data structures (not just key-value)
17	- Built-in pub/sub for real-time messaging
18	- Persistence options (RDB snapshots, AOF logs)
19	- Replication and high availability out of the box
20	- Widely adopted — used by Twitter, GitHub, Stack Overflow, etc.
21	
22	## Core Data Structures
23	
24	- **String**: simplest type, can store text, numbers, or binary (max 512MB)
25	- **List**: ordered collection, supports push/pop from both ends (queues, stacks)
26	- **Set**: unordered unique elements, supports unions, intersections, diffs
27	- **Sorted Set (ZSet)**: like Set but each member has a score, auto-sorted
28	- **Hash**: field-value pairs inside a single key (like a mini object/document)
29	- **Stream**: append-only log, used for event sourcing and message queues
30	
31	## Basic Commands
32	
33	```
34	SET key value              # store a string
35	GET key                    # retrieve a string
36	DEL key                    # delete a key
37	EXPIRE key seconds         # set TTL
38	TTL key                    # check remaining TTL
39	
40	LPUSH list value           # push to head of list
41	RPOP list                  # pop from tail
42	
43	SADD set member            # add to set
44	SMEMBERS set               # list all members
45	
46	HSET hash field value      # set field in hash
47	HGETALL hash               # get all fields
48	
49	ZADD zset score member     # add to sorted set
50	ZRANGE zset 0 -1           # get all sorted
51	```
52	
53	## Architecture
54	
55	```
56	Client ──► Redis Server (single-threaded event loop)
57	                │
58	                ├── Memory (primary storage)
59	                ├── RDB (periodic snapshots to disk)
60	                ├── AOF (append-only log to disk)
61	                └── Replication → Replica nodes
62	```
63	
64	- Single-threaded for commands — no locks, no race conditions
65	- I/O multiplexing handles thousands of concurrent connections
66	- Background threads handle persistence and slow operations
67	
68	## Topics
69	
70	- [[DistributedSystems/Persistence|Persistence (RDB & AOF)]]
71	- [[DistributedSystems/Replication|Replication]]
72	- [[DistributedSystems/PubSub|Pub/Sub]]
73	- [[DistributedSystems/TransactionsPipeline|Transactions & Pipeline]]
74	- [[DistributedSystems/SecurityAuth|Security & Auth]]
75	- [[DistributedSystems/TLS|TLS]]
76	- [[DistributedSystems/Performance|Performance]]
77	- [[DistributedSystems/MemoryManagement|Memory Management]]
78	- [[DistributedSystems/Concurrency|Concurrency]]
79	- [[DistributedSystems/DataStructures|Data Structures]]
80	- [[DistributedSystems/ExpirationTTL|Expiration & TTL]]
81	
82	## Use Cases
83	
84	- **Caching**: session storage, API response caching, page caching
85	- **Rate Limiting**: track request counts with INCR + EXPIRE
86	- **Leaderboards**: sorted sets with scores
87	- **Real-time Chat**: pub/sub channels
88	- **Job Queues**: lists with LPUSH/BRPOP pattern
89	- **Session Store**: fast read/write for user sessions
90	
91	---
92	
93	## Session Management — How It Works in Practice
94	
95	Redis does not have tables or SQL. The "link" to the user is built into the key name itself:
96	
97	```
98	key:   "session:token:abc32323"   ← identifies whose session it is
99	value: "123"                       ← the user ID
100	TTL:   3600 seconds                ← expires automatically
101	```
102	
103	### Full Flow
104	
105	**Login:**
106	```
107	User logs in with correct password
108	→ server generates a random token: "abc32323"
109	→ SET session:token:abc32323 "123" EX 3600
110	→ returns token to the browser (cookie/header)
111	```
112	
113	**Every request:**
114	```
115	Browser sends token "abc32323"
116	→ server asks Redis: GET session:token:abc32323
117	→ Redis returns: "123" (the user ID)
118	→ authenticated ✓
119	
120	If Redis returns nil → session expired or never existed → 401
121	```
122	
123	**Logout:**
124	```
125	DEL session:token:abc32323
126	→ next request: GET session:token:abc32323 → nil → not authenticated
127	```
128	
129	### Middleware Pattern (PHP)
130	
131	The session check does not live inside each action — it lives in a single file included everywhere. One check runs before anything else on every protected page.
132	
133	```php
134	// config.php — included in every protected page
135	
136	$redis = new Redis();
137	$redis->connect('127.0.0.1', 6379);
138	
139	$token = $_COOKIE['token'] ?? null;
140	
141	if (!$token) {
142	    header('Location: /login.php');
143	    exit;
144	}
145	
146	$user_id = $redis->get("session:token:$token");
147	
148	if (!$user_id) {
149	    header('Location: /login.php');
150	    exit;
151	}
152	
153	// if we got here, $user_id is available for the entire page
154	```
155	
156	Every protected page:
157	```php
158	<?php
159	require_once 'config.php';  // ← check done, $user_id available
160	
161	// rest of the page normally
162	echo "Welcome, user $user_id";
163	?>
164	```
165	
166	The login page is the only one that does **not** include this file — or includes it without the redirect, since the user does not have a session yet.
167	
168	> Redis does not notify you when a session expires — it just returns nil. Your application decides what to do: redirect to login, return 401, etc.
169	
170	## Resources
171	
172	- https://redis.io/docs
173	- https://try.redis.io (interactive tutorial)
174	- https://university.redis.io (free courses)
175	
176	#### My commentaries
177	-  
178

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Replication
2	
3	Replication is the process of keeping data synchronized across multiple nodes. A primary node receives writes and one or more replicas copy the data.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## Core Terms
8	
9	- **Primary (master):** node that accepts writes.
10	- **Replica:** node that receives a copy of data from the primary.
11	- **Replication stream:** ordered sequence of changes sent from primary to replicas.
12	- **Offset:** current position in the replication stream.
13	- **Backlog:** buffer of recent changes kept by the primary.
14	
15	---
16	
17	## Offsets
18	
19	An **offset** is the position in the replication stream — a single integer that grows monotonically as commands are processed.
20	
21	- Primary keeps a current offset (advances with every write)
22	- Each replica tracks its own offset (advances as it applies commands)
23	- If a replica reconnects, it sends its last known offset to the primary
24	
25	The primary then compares offsets:
26	- Gap is small and still in backlog → **partial resync** (send only the missing commands)
27	- Gap is too large or offset not found → **full resync** (send a full RDB snapshot)
28	
29	This allows incremental catch-up instead of always copying everything from scratch.
30	
31	### How the offset works step by step
32	
33	```
34	Primary offset: 100
35	
36	  [write: SET name "João"]  → primary offset becomes 101
37	  [write: SET age 25]       → primary offset becomes 102
38	  [write: SET name "Maria"] → primary offset becomes 103
39	
40	Replica was at offset 101 and reconnects:
41	  → asks: "give me everything after 101"
42	  → primary sends: commands at 102 and 103
43	  → replica applies them, now also at offset 103 ✓
44	```
45	
46	### Why offset and not a timestamp?
47	
48	Timestamps have problems in distributed systems — clocks drift between machines. An offset is just a counter: deterministic, unambiguous, and always increasing. There is no disagreement about what "103" means.
49	
50	---
51	
52	## Backlog
53	
54	A **backlog** is a buffer of recent changes kept by the primary.
55	
56	- Stores the last N bytes/commands of the replication stream
57	- Enables replicas to catch up after a short disconnect
58	- If the replica is too far behind, a full resync is required
59	
60	Backlog size is a trade-off: memory vs recovery speed.
61	
62	---
63	
64	## Full Resync vs Partial Resync
65	
66	- **Partial resync:** replica requests missing data starting at its offset
67	- **Full resync:** replica discards state and copies a full snapshot
68	
69	Partial resync is faster and cheaper, but only works if the backlog still contains the missing data.
70	
71	---
72	
73	## Minimal Flow (Concept)
74	
75	1) Replica connects to primary
76	2) Replica sends its last known offset
77	3) Primary decides:
78	   - If backlog covers the gap -> send missing data (partial resync)
79	   - Else -> send full snapshot (full resync)
80	4) Replica applies changes and updates its offset
81	
82	---
83	
84	## Failure Scenarios
85	
86	- **Network drop:** replica reconnects using its last offset
87	- **Long outage:** backlog too small -> full resync
88	- **Lag:** replica behind -> reads may be stale
89	
90	## Failover
91	
92	Failover is when the primary dies and a replica automatically takes its place.
93	
94	```
95	Before:
96	Primary (alive)  →  Replica
97	
98	Primary dies ↓
99	
100	After:
101	Primary (dead)      Replica → promoted to new Primary
102	```
103	
104	### How it works
105	
106	1. Primary stops responding
107	2. The system detects it is down
108	3. The replica with the least lag is **promoted** to primary
109	4. The application starts writing to it
110	5. When the old primary comes back, it becomes a replica of the new one
111	
112	### Without failover vs with failover
113	
114	| | **Without failover** | **With failover** |
115	|---|---|---|
116	| Primary dies | App stops until someone fixes it manually | App recovers in seconds, automatically |
117	| Human needed? | Yes | No |
118	
119	### Who manages failover in Redis
120	
121	- **Redis Sentinel** — monitors the primary, triggers failover, notifies the app of the new primary address
122	- **Redis Cluster** — built-in failover as part of the cluster protocol
123	
124	> Replication is the mechanism. Failover is what replication makes possible.
125	
126	---
127	
128	## When to Use Replication
129	
130	Replication makes sense when:
131	
132	- **Data loss has real cost** — shopping carts, queues, application state that cannot be trivially rebuilt
133	- **Read throughput needs to scale** — distribute reads across replicas, keep writes on the primary
134	- **Availability matters** — if the primary goes down, a replica takes over and the system keeps running
135	
136	A single Redis instance is fine when the data is purely a disposable cache — if it dies, you rebuild from the source of truth elsewhere.
137	
138	> Set up replication when the cost of losing the data exceeds the cost of running two instances.
139	
140	## Replication vs Horizontal Scaling
141	
142	These are often confused but solve different problems:
143	
144	| | **Replication** | **Horizontal Scaling** |
145	|---|---|---|
146	| What is copied | Data (state) | The application (logic) |
147	| Example | Redis primary + replicas | 10 containers of your backend |
148	| Why | Survival + read scaling | Throughput + availability |
149	| Needs sync? | Yes — replicas must stay in sync | No — each instance is independent |
150	
151	Your **backend** (the application) you scale by adding more instances — no replication needed, because it is **stateless**: it does not hold state, it just processes requests. Any instance can handle any request.
152	
153	Your **Redis** you replicate because it is **stateful**: it *is* the state. If it dies without a replica, the data is gone.
154	
155	> Stateless things you scale. Stateful things you replicate.
156	
157	### Horizontal Scaling in Practice
158	
159	When your backend is under heavy load, you scale it by launching more instances — more containers, more processes, more machines. This works because the backend is **stateless**: every request carries everything it needs (token, body, params), so any instance can handle any request.
160	
161	```
162	Before:
163	[Container 1 - Backend]  ← overloaded
164	
165	After:
166	[Container 1 - Backend]
167	[Container 2 - Backend]  ← same app, more instances
168	[Container 3 - Backend]
169	         ↑
170	    Load Balancer distributes requests across them
171	```
172	
173	State lives **outside** the containers:
174	
175	```
176	[Container 1]  ↘
177	[Container 2]  →  Redis (sessions, cache)  +  PostgreSQL (data)
178	[Container 3]  ↗
179	```
180	
181	All containers read and write to the same Redis and the same database. None of them store anything locally.
182	
183	This is exactly why Redis replication matters at scale — if 3 containers depend on a single Redis and it dies, all 3 stop. Replication ensures Redis survives.
184	
185	> You scale the backend horizontally because it is stateless.
186	> You replicate Redis because it carries the state of all of them.
187	
188	## Design Notes
189	
190	- Offsets are sequence positions, not timestamps
191	- Replication is often async; replicas can lag behind
192	- If backlog is smaller than the write rate over the disconnect window, full resync is inevitable
193	- These concepts (offset, backlog, partial/full resync) only matter when something breaks — on the happy path, the primary just streams commands to replicas continuously and offsets stay in sync
194	
195	---
196	
197	## Minimal Protocol Sketch
198	
199	- Replica -> Primary: `PSYNC <replica_id> <offset>`
200	- Primary -> Replica: `+CONTINUE <offset>` or `+FULLRESYNC <offset>`
201	- Primary -> Replica: stream of writes (each write advances offset)
202	---
203	
204	## Related Notes
205	
206	- [[Persistence]] — RDB, AOF, e como se conectam com replicação
207	- [[Concurrency]]
208	- [[TCP]]
209

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# Security & Auth
2	
3	Security in distributed systems is about two fundamental questions:
4	
5	- **Who are you?** → Authentication
6	- **What can you do?** → Authorization
7	
8	---
9	
10	## Authentication
11	
12	Authentication is the act of proving your identity before being allowed in.
13	
14	The most common forms:
15	
16	| Method | How it works |
17	|---|---|
18	| Password | You know a secret string |
19	| Token (JWT) | You hold a signed token issued by the server |
20	| API Key | You hold a key tied to your identity |
21	| SSH Key | You hold a private key that matches a public key on the server |
22	| Certificate (mTLS) | Your machine holds a cryptographic certificate |
23	
24	**Analogy:** the bouncer at the door — you only get in if you can prove who you are.
25	
26	Authentication answers: **"are you who you say you are?"**
27	
28	### SSH Keys
29	
30	SSH keys work as a key pair:
31	
32	```
33	Private key  ← stays on your machine, never leaves
34	Public key   ← you give to the server upfront
35	```
36	
37	When you try to connect:
38	
39	```
40	You        →  "I want in"
41	Server     →  sends an encrypted challenge
42	You        →  deciphers it with your private key and responds
43	Server     →  only the private key holder could do that → allowed in ✓
44	```
45	
46	You never send a password over the network — you prove your identity by solving a cryptographic challenge.
47	
48	**Analogy:** a padlock with two keys. You give the open padlock to the server. Only you have the key to open it. If you can open it, you proved you are you.
49	
50	| | **Password** | **SSH Key** |
51	|---|---|---|
52	| What travels over the network | The password (even if encrypted) | Nothing sensitive — only the proof |
53	| Can be stolen remotely | Yes | No — the private key never leaves your machine |
54	| Brute force possible | Yes | Practically impossible |
55	
56	---
57	
58	## Authorization
59	
60	Authorization happens after authentication — it defines what an authenticated identity is allowed to do.
61	
62	You can be authenticated (proven who you are) but still not authorized to do certain things.
63	
64	**Analogy:** you have a keycard to enter the building *(authentication)*, but your keycard only opens the front door and your floor — not the server room *(authorization)*.
65	
66	Authorization answers: **"are you allowed to do this?"**
67	
68	---
69	
70	## ACL (Access Control List)
71	
72	An ACL is the most common way to implement authorization — a list that maps identities to permissions.
73	
74	```
75	user: analytics  → can read  user:* keys
76	user: api        → can read and write user:* keys
77	user: admin      → can do everything
78	```
79	
80	Each entry says: *this identity can do these actions on these resources.*
81	
82	**Principle of least privilege:** give each user the minimum access required to do its job — nothing more. If a service is compromised, the blast radius is contained.
83	
84	---
85	
86	## Roles
87	
88	Instead of assigning permissions to each user individually, you define **roles** — named bundles of permissions — and assign roles to users.
89	
90	```
91	Role: readonly   → GET, LIST
92	Role: readwrite  → GET, LIST, SET, DELETE
93	Role: admin      → everything
94	
95	User: analytics  → role: readonly
96	User: api        → role: readwrite
97	User: dylan      → role: admin
98	```
99	
100	This makes it easy to manage permissions at scale — change the role, all users with that role are updated.
101	
102	**Analogy:** job titles in a company. An intern has intern-level access. A manager has manager-level access. You define what each title can do, then assign titles to people.
103	
104	---
105	
106	## Authentication vs Authorization
107	
108	A common source of confusion:
109	
110	| | **Authentication** | **Authorization** |
111	|---|---|---|
112	| Question | Who are you? | What can you do? |
113	| Happens | First | After authentication |
114	| Example | Login with password | Can you access this endpoint? |
115	| If it fails | "I don't know you" | "I know you, but you can't do this" |
116	
117	You cannot have authorization without authentication — you need to know who someone is before deciding what they can do.
118	
119	---
120	
121	## TLS — Encrypting the Connection
122	
123	Authentication proves who you are. TLS encrypts what travels between client and server.
124	
125	Without TLS, passwords and data travel as plain text — any router in the path can read everything. With TLS, an encrypted tunnel is created and anyone who intercepts traffic sees only noise.
126	
127	> See [[TLS]] for the full breakdown — handshake, Diffie-Hellman key exchange, certificates, and how it layers on top of TCP.
128	
129	---
130	a## In Redis
131	
132	Redis implements these concepts natively:
133	
134	- **Authentication** → `AUTH password` or username + password
135	- **Authorization** → ACL system with per-user command and key permissions
136	- **TLS** → encrypted connections between clients and server
137	
138	```bash
139	# create a user with limited access
140	ACL SETUSER analytics on >secret ~user:* +GET
141	
142	# user can only GET keys starting with user:
143	# cannot SET, DEL, FLUSHALL, or access other keys
144	```
145	
146	Redis does not have built-in named roles — you simulate them by creating users with matching permission sets.
147	
148	---
149	
150	## Design Notes
151	
152	- Always authenticate in production — never leave systems open
153	- Use roles to manage permissions at scale instead of per-user rules
154	- Principle of least privilege reduces damage when something is compromised
155	- TLS is mandatory when traffic crosses a network you do not fully control
156	- Audit who has access regularly — remove what is no longer needed
157	
158	---
159	
160	## Related Notes
161	
162	- [[Replication]]
163	- [[Kubernetes]]
164	- [[TransactionsPipeline]]
165

toolCalls.name: Read
toolCalls.result:
1	# TLS (Transport Layer Security)
2	
3	TLS is the protocol that creates an **encrypted tunnel** between two parties over a network. Nobody in the middle can read or alter what travels through it.
4	
5	It runs on top of TCP — TCP opens the connection, TLS secures it.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Three Goals
10	
11	1. **Identity** — verify the server is who it claims to be (certificate)
12	2. **Key exchange** — agree on a secret key without ever sending it over the network
13	3. **Encryption** — encrypt everything from that point on
14	
15	---
16	
17	## The TLS Handshake Step by Step
18	
19	### Step 1 — ClientHello
20
